name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱهْدِنَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (٦)
```

```english
Guide us on the straight path, (6)
```

```arabic
صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٧)
```

```english
the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray. (7)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** اهدنا الصراط المستقيم صراط الذين انعمت عليهم الخ (Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed...); as for guidance (الهداية) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — its meaning will become clear in the discussion regarding the path (الصراط). As for the path (الصراط), it is nearly synonymous with the road (الطريق) and the way (السبيل). The Exalted has described the path (الصراط) as being straight (الاستقامة), and then clarified that it is the path traversed by those whom Allah the Exalted has blessed. Thus, the path characterized by this is the one for which guidance is sought; it represents the ultimate goal of worship (العبادة) — *the act of total submission and devotion to Allah* — meaning that the servant asks his Lord that his sincere worship be situated within this path.

To clarify this: Allah, the Glorified, has established in His speech for the human species—indeed, for all besides Him—a way (سبيلاً) by which they travel toward Him, the Glorified. The Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ** — "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord with [untiring] effort, and you will meet Him." (84:6). And the Exalted said: **وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ** — "and toward Him is the return." (64:3). And He said: **أَلَا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ** — "Look! To Allah do all matters return." (42:53). These and other verses clearly indicate that everyone is a traveler on a way, and that they are all journeying toward Allah, the Glorified.

Then He clarified that the way is not a single way with a single attribute; rather, it branches into two directions and is divided into two roads. He said: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. And worship Me. This is a straight path’?" (36:60-61).

Thus, there is a straight path (صراط مستقيم) and another road beyond it. The Exalted said: **فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا لِي وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا بِي لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ** — "I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me. So let them respond to Me, and let them have faith in Me, so that they may fare rightly." (2:186). And the Exalted said: **ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ** — "Call Me, I will answer you! Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased." (40:60). The Exalted thus clarified that He is near to His servants, and that the nearest road to Him is the road of His worship and supplication. Then the Exalted said, describing those who do not believe: **أُولَٰئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِن مَّكَانٍ بَعِيدٍ** — "They are being called from a distant place." (41:44). He thus clarified that the destination of those who do not believe in their journey and way is distant.

It thus becomes clear that the way to Allah consists of two ways: a near way, which is the way of the believers (المؤمنين), and a distant way, which is the way of others. This is one type of difference in the way. There is another type of difference; the Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْهَا لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْاوَابُ السَّمَاءِ** — "Indeed those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, the gates of the heaven will not be opened for them." (7:40). Were there no path for a traveler, the "gate" would have no meaning; thus, there is a road from the lower realm to the higher realm. The Exalted also said: **وَمَن يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِي فَقَدْ هَوَىٰ** — "and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes." (20:81). Perishing (الهوي) here implies falling to the lowest depths; thus, there is another road leading toward baseness and descent. The Exalted said: **وَمَن يَتَبَدَّلِ الْكُفْرَ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَاءَ السَّبِيلِ** — "Whoever exchanges faith for unfaith has certainly strayed from the right way." (2:108). He defined straying from the right way as polytheism (الشرك) — *the act of associating partners with Allah in His essence, attributes, or acts* — by virtue of His saying: "has certainly strayed." At that point, people are divided in their roads into three categories: those whose road leads upward, who are those who believe in the signs of Allah and are not too proud to worship Him; those whose road leads downward, who are those who have earned [His] wrath (المغضوب عليهم); and those who have lost the road and are wandering aimlessly within it, who are the astray (الضالون). Perhaps this tripartite division is alluded to in His saying: صراط الذين انعمت عليهم غير المغضوب عليهم ولا الضالين (The path of those whom You have blessed, such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray).

The straight path (الصراط المستقيم) is inevitably not the other two roads among the three—namely, the road of those who have incurred wrath and the road of the astray. Therefore, it belongs to the first road, which is the road of the believers who are not arrogant. However, His saying: **يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ** — "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank." (58:11) indicates that the first road itself also contains a division.

The explanation for this is that every instance of straying (ضلال) is polytheism (شرك), and vice versa, as you have recognized from His saying: **وَمَن يَتَبَدَّلِ الْكُفْرَ بِالْإِيمَانِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَاءَ السَّبِيلِ** — "Whoever exchanges faith for unfaith has certainly strayed from the right way." (2:108). In this same vein is His saying: **أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيرًا** — "‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. And worship Me. This is a straight path.’ He has certainly led astray a great multitude of you." (36:60-62). The Quran considers polytheism (الشرك) to be injustice (الظلم), and vice versa, as indicated by His saying, narrating the words of Satan when the matter is decided: **إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِن قَبْلُ إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَدَابٌ أَلِيمٌ** — "I disbelieve in your having associated me with Allah before. Indeed for the wrongdoers there is a painful punishment." (14:22). Similarly, He considers injustice (الظلم) to be straying (ضلال) in His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمَانَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ** — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided." (6:82). This is evident from the way guidance (الاهتداء) and safety (الأمن) from straying or the punishment that follows it are made contingent upon the removal of injustice and not tainting faith with it. In short, straying, polytheism, and injustice are essentially one thing; they are inseparable in their external reality (مصداقاً). This is what we mean when we say that each of them defines the other or *is* the other—referring to the external reality (المصداق) rather than the conceptual meaning (المفهوم).

Once you recognize this, you will know that the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), which is the path of those who are not astray, is a path in which no polytheism or injustice occurs whatsoever, just as no straying occurs within it. This applies neither to the inner heart (باطن الجنان) in the form of disbelief (كفر) or thoughts that do not please Allah, the Glorified, nor to the outward limbs (ظاهر الجوارح) in the form of committing a sin (معصية) or falling short in obedience. This is the true essence of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in both knowledge and action, for there is no third option; and what is there beyond the truth except straying? This corresponds to His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمَانَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ** — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided." (6:82). In this verse, there is an affirmation of safety (الأمن) on the path and a promise of complete guidance, based on what scholars have mentioned: that the active participle (اسم الفاعل) truly refers to the future. Let this be understood, for this is one of the attributes of the straight path.

Furthermore, He has identified those blessed ones to whom He attributed the straight path by saying: **وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولَٰئِكَ رَفِيقًا** — "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69). He described this faith and obedience prior to this verse by saying: **فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيمًا وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ اقْتُلُوا أَنفُسَكُمْ أَوِ اخْرُجُوا مِن دِيَارِكُم مَّا فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِّنْهُمْ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا مَا يُوعَظُونَ بِهِ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتًا** — "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in full submission. Had We decreed for them, ‘Slay yourselves,’ or ‘Leave your homes,’ they would not have done it except a few of them. And had they done what they were advised, it would have been better for them and more confirming." (4:65-66). Thus, He described them as having complete steadfastness in word and deed, outwardly and inwardly, in their servitude (العبودية); not a single one of them deviates in this regard. Despite this, He placed these believers as followers of those blessed ones, in a rank below theirs, due to the word "with" (مع) and His saying: "and excellent companions are they!" He did not say: "then they are *among* those..."

Similar to this verse is His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَدَاءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ** — "Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord; they shall have their reward and their light." (57:19). This refers to the joining of the believers with the martyrs and the truthful in the Hereafter, due to His saying: "with their Lord," and His saying: "they shall have their reward."

Therefore, those (the people of the straight path) are higher in status and more elevated in degree and station than these—the believers who have purified their hearts and actions from straying, polytheism, and injustice. Contemplating these verses leads to the certain conclusion that these believers, despite their high status, still have a remaining stage; if it were completed in them, they would become among those whom Allah has blessed, and they would rise from the station of companionship with them to the degree of being included among them. Perhaps this stage is a type of knowledge (العلم) of Allah, which He mentioned in His saying: **يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ** — "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank." (58:11). Thus, the people of the straight path are blessed with a blessing that is the highest of all blessings in value, surpassing the blessing of complete faith. This, too, is one of the attributes of the straight path.

Furthermore, although He repeatedly mentions the path (الصراط) and the way (السبيل) in His speech, He does not attribute to Himself more than one straight path (صراط مستقيم), while He attributes to Himself many ways (سبلاً). The Mightiest of speakers said: **وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا** — "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways." (29:69). Similarly, He did not attribute the straight path to any of His creation except in this verse (the path of those whom You have blessed), but He did attribute the way (السبيل) to others among His creation. The Exalted said: **قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ** — "Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight...’" (12:108). And the Exalted said: **سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَيَّ** — "the way of him who turns to Me in penitence." (31:15). And He said: **سَبِيلِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "the way of the faithful." (4:115). From this, it is known that the way (السبيل) is different from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم); for the way varies, multiplies, and increases according to the differences among the worshipers who traverse the way of worship, unlike the straight path. This is alluded to in His saying: **قَدْ جَاءَكُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَابٌ مُّبِينٌ يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوَانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلَامِ وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** — "Certainly there has come to you a light from Allah, and a manifest Book. With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path." (5:15-16). Thus, He counted the ways as many and the path as one. This straight path is either the collection of these many ways, or these ways lead to it by connecting to one another and uniting within it.

Also, the Exalted said: **وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ** — "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him." (12:106). He clarified that there is a type of polytheism (which is straying) that can coexist with faith, and that is a way (سبيل). From this, it is known that the way can coexist with polytheism, but the straight path cannot coexist with straying, as He said: "nor are astray" (ولا الضالين).

Contemplating these verses reveals that each of these ways may coexist with some deficiency or distinction, unlike the straight path. Each of them is the straight path, yet it is distinct from the others and separate from them; however, the straight path unites with each of them while simultaneously uniting with what opposes it. This is inferred from some of the aforementioned verses and others, such as His saying: **وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ** — "And worship Me. This is a straight path." (36:61). And the Exalted's saying: **قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدَانِي رَبِّي إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ دِينًا قِيَمًا مِّلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا** — "Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path: the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif...’" (6:161). He called worship a straight path and called the religion a straight path, and both are common to all the ways. Thus, the relationship of the straight path to the ways of Allah is like the relationship of the soul (الروح) to the body (البدن). Just as the body has various stages in its life—being different at each stage, such as infancy, childhood, adolescence, youth, middle age, old age, and decrepitude—the soul remains the soul and is united with these stages. The body may undergo states that conflict with what the soul loves and requires if left to itself, unlike the soul, which is the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — of Allah upon which He created mankind. Yet the body, despite this, is the soul—meaning the human being. Similarly, the way to Allah is the straight path, except that a way—such as the way of the believers, the way of those who turn in penitence, or the way of those who follow the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or other ways of Allah—might be afflicted by an external malady or deficiency. However, these do not occur to the straight path. As you have seen, faith—which is a way—may coexist with polytheism and straying, but the straight path does not coexist with any of that. Thus, the way has many levels in terms of its purity or mixture, its nearness or distance, and all of them are upon the straight path, or they *are* it.

Allah, the Glorified, has explained this meaning—namely, the variation of the ways to Allah despite all of them being part of His straight path—in a parable He struck for truth and falsehood in His speech. The Exalted said: **أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغَاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ** — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries off a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire to make ornaments or wares, there is a scum like it. Thus does Allah draw the parable of truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth. Thus does Allah draw the parables." (13:17). He clarified that hearts and understandings differ in their reception of knowledge and perfection, even though all of them rely upon and terminate at a single heavenly provision. The full discussion of this parable will come in Surah al-Ra'd. In short, this is also one of the attributes of the straight path.

If you reflect upon the preceding attributes of the straight path, it will become clear to you that the straight path is dominant (مهيمنٌ) over all the ways to Allah and the roads leading to Him. This means that a way to Allah is only a way for Him and leads to Him to the extent that it actually contains the straight path, while the straight path is a guide that leads to Him absolutely and without condition or restriction. For this reason, Allah the Exalted called it a "straight path" (صراطاً مستقيماً). For the path (الصراط) is the clear road, derived from "I swallowed" (سرطت سرطاً) when one gulps something down; it is as if it swallows those who traverse it, not allowing them to leave it nor expelling them from its interior. The "straight" (المستقيم) is that which seeks to stand on its own leg, thus mastering itself and what belongs to it, like one who stands and is in control of his affairs. The meaning returns to it being that whose state does not change and whose condition does not vary. Thus, the straight path is that whose judgment in guiding and bringing its travelers to their goal and destination never fails. The Exalted said: **فأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِهِ فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِي رَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلٍ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِرَاطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا** — "As for those who have faith in Allah, and hold fast to Him, He will admit them to His mercy and bounty, and He will guide them to Himself by a straight path." (4:175). That is, the matter of this guidance does not fail; rather, it remains as it is always. And the Exalted said: **فَمَن يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا كَأَنَّمَا يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّمَاءِ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللَّهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ وَهَٰذَا صِرَاطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيمًا** — "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing to a height. Thus does Allah lay abomination on those who do not have faith. This is the straight path of your Lord." (6:125-126). That is, this is His method which neither varies nor fails. And the Exalted said: **قَالَ هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ** — "He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me. Indeed as for My servants, you do not have any authority over them, except the perverse who follow you.’" (15:41-42). That is, this is My sunnah (سنتي) and My way always, without change. It follows the same course as His saying: **فَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَحْوِيلًا** — "you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent (sunnah), and you will never find any revision in Allah’s precedent." (35:43).

Several matters have become clear from what we have mentioned regarding the meaning of the straight path:

**First:** The roads to Allah differ in perfection and deficiency, and in being high or low, in terms of their proximity to the source of reality and the straight path—such as Islam, faith (الايمان), worship (العبادة), sincerity (الاخلاص), and humble submission (الإخبات). Similarly, their opposites—such as disbelief (الكفر), polytheism (الشرك), denial (الجحود), rebellion (الطغيان), and disobedience (المعصية)—are also varied. The Glorified said: **وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَاتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمَالَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ** — "For everyone there are degrees [of reward or punishment] according to what they have done, and so that He may pay them in full for their deeds, and they will not be wronged." (46:19).

This is analogous to the divine knowledges (المعارف الالهية) received by intellects from Allah; they differ according to the variation in predispositions (الاستعدادات) and are colored by the hues of capacities (القابليات), as conveyed by the parable struck in His saying: **أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا** — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity..." (13:17).

**Second:** Just as the straight path is dominant over all ways, so too are its people—those whom Allah the Exalted has established within it, taken charge of their affairs, and entrusted with the guidance of His servants, where He said: "and excellent companions are they!" (4:69). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلَاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكَاةَ وَهُمْ رَاكِعُونَ** — "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while bowing down." (5:55). This verse was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), according to mutawatir (متواترة) — *narrations transmitted by so many chains that their authenticity is certain* — reports. He (peace be upon him) was the first to open this door from this Ummah. The full discussion of this verse will follow.

**Third:** The meaning of guidance (الهداية) to the path is determined by the determination of the path's own meaning. To clarify: guidance is "pointing the way" (الدلالة) as stated in *al-Sihah*. It also mentions that making it transitive to two objects is the dialect of the people of Hijaz, while others make it transitive to the second object using the preposition "to" (إلى). His view is the apparent one. It has been said that if guidance is transitive to the second object by itself, it means "conveying to the goal" (الايصال إلى المطلوب), and if it is transitive via "to" (إلى), it means "showing the way" (إرائة الطريق). This is argued using verses such as: **إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَن يَشَاءُ** — "You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes." (28:56). Since guidance in the sense of showing the way is established [for the Prophet], what is negated must be something else, which is conveying to the goal. The Exalted said: **وَهَدَيْنَاهُمَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** — "and We guided them to the straight path." (37:118). And the Exalted said: **وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** — "and you indeed guide to a straight path." (42:52).

Thus, guidance by conveying to the goal is transitive to the second object by itself, and guidance by showing the way is via "to" (إلى). However, the truth is that the aforementioned negation is a negation of the reality of guidance which is subsistent through Allah the Exalted, not a negation of it entirely. In other words, it is a negation of perfection rather than a negation of the reality. Furthermore, this is refuted by His saying, narrating the words of the believer from the family of Pharaoh: **يَا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ الرَّشَادِ** — "O my people! Follow me, I will guide you to the way of rectitude." (40:38). Thus, the truth is that the meaning of guidance does not differ with the variation in transitivity. It is possible that the transitivity to the second object is like their saying, "I entered the house" (دخلت الدار).

In short, guidance (الهداية) is pointing the way and showing the goal by showing the road, and it is a form of conveying to the goal. It only comes from Allah, the Glorified, and His sunnah is the sunnah of causes (الأسباب)—by bringing about a cause through which the goal is uncovered and the servant's arrival at his destination in his journey is realized. Allah, the Glorified, has explained this by saying: **فَمَن يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ** — "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam." (6:125). And His saying: **ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَقُلُوبُهُمْ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى اللَّهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ** — "then their skins and their hearts soften to the remembrance of Allah. That is Allah’s guidance: He guides with it whomever He wishes." (39:23). The transitivity of "soften" (تلين) with "to" (إلى) is due to it implying meanings like inclination and tranquility. Thus, it is His creation of an attribute in the heart by which it accepts the remembrance of Allah and inclines and finds tranquility in it. Just as His ways are varied, so too does guidance vary according to the ways to which it is attributed; for every way, there is a guidance preceding it that is specific to it.

This variation is alluded to in His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous." (29:69). For there is a difference between a servant striving *in the way* of Allah and striving *in* Allah. The striver in the first case seeks the safety of the way and the removal of obstacles from it, unlike the striver in the second case, who seeks only the face of Allah. Allah, the Glorified, then aids him with guidance to one way rather than another according to his specific predisposition. Similarly, Allah aids him with guidance to way after way until He reserves him for Himself, exalted is His greatness.

**Fourth:** Since the straight path was a matter preserved within the ways of Allah despite their varying levels and degrees, it is correct for Allah to guide a person to it even if he is already guided. Thus, He guides him from path to path, meaning He guides him to one of His ways and then increases his guidance so that he is guided from that way to one that is higher in degree. His saying: إهدنا الصراط (Guide us on the path)—which He, the Exalted, narrates from one whom He has already guided through worship—is of this nature. The objection that seeking guidance from one who is already guided is seeking to acquire what is already possessed (تحصيل الحاصل), which is impossible, does not apply. Similarly, the objection that treading the path after it is already assumed to be trodden is acquiring what is already possessed and cannot be the subject of a request—the answer to this is clear.

Likewise, the objection that our Sharia is more complete and broader in all respects than the Sharias of previous nations, so what is the meaning of asking Allah, the Glorified, to guide us to the path of those whom Allah blessed from among them? This is because a Sharia being more complete than another Sharia is one matter, and the one who holds fast to a Sharia being more complete than the one who holds fast to another Sharia is a different matter altogether. For an ordinary believer among the followers of the Sharia of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—despite his Sharia being more complete and broader—is not more complete than Noah or Abraham (peace be upon them), despite their Sharias being older and earlier. This is only because the ruling of Sharias and acting upon them is different from the ruling of the guardianship (الولاية) — *the spiritual authority and proximity to Allah* — resulting from being established within them and characterized by them. Thus, the possessor of the station of pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص), even if he was from the people of previous Sharias, is more complete and superior to one who has not attained the station of monotheism and in whose soul the life of gnosis (المعرفة) has not settled, and in whose heart the light of divine guidance has not been established, even if he acts according to the Muhammadan Sharia (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), which is the most complete and broadest of Sharias. Therefore, it is permissible for the possessor of a lower station from the people of the complete Sharia to seek guidance and ask Allah for guidance to the station of the possessor of a high station from the people of a Sharia that is below it.

Among the most peculiar things mentioned in this regard is what some researchers among the people of exegesis stated in response to this doubt: that the religion of Allah is one, which is Islam, and the fundamental knowledges—monotheism, prophethood, and the Resurrection—and the universal knowledges branching from them are one in all Sharias. The only merit of this Sharia over those preceding it is that the subsidiary rulings (الاحكام الفرعية) within it are broader and more inclusive of all affairs of life; thus, it has more concern for preserving the interests of the servants. Furthermore, the foundation of this Sharia is based on demonstration through all its methods: wisdom, goodly exhortation, and best dispute. Then, although the religion is one and the universal knowledges are equal in all, they traversed the way of their Lord before we did and preceded us in that; so Allah commanded us to look into what they were upon and take heed of what they became. This is what he said.

**I say:** This discourse is built upon principles in the method of exegesis that oppose the principles upon which the method of exegesis must be built. It is built on the premise that the realities of the fundamental knowledges are one in terms of reality, without variation in levels and degrees. Similarly, all other inner spiritual perfections are the same; thus, the most superior of the near prophets and the lowest of the believers are equal in terms of existence and its external ontological perfection. Superiority is only according to the stations established by legislative decree (الجعل التشريعي) without relying on ontology (تكوين), just as the superiority between a king and a subject is only according to the established conventional station without any difference in terms of human existence. This is his view.

This principle has another principle upon which it is built: the doctrine of the primacy of matter (اصالة المادة) and the denial of primacy to what is beyond it, or remaining non-committal regarding it except for Allah, the Glorified, by way of exception through evidence. Those who have fallen into this pitfall did so for one of two reasons: either the doctrine of sufficiency with the senses, relying on material sciences, or the abandonment of contemplation (التدبر) in the Quran by sufficing with exegesis based on common understanding.

This discourse has a long sequel which we will present in some of the upcoming scholarly discussions, if Allah the Exalted wills.

**Fifth:** The merit of the people of the straight path over others, as well as their path over the ways of others, is only through knowledge (العلم), not action (العمل). For they possess a knowledge of the station of their Lord that others do not. It has become clear from what preceded that complete action exists in some of the ways that are below their path; thus, nothing remains for their merit except knowledge. As for what this knowledge is and how it is, we shall discuss it, if Allah wills, regarding His saying: **أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا** — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity..." (13:17).

This meaning is alluded to in His saying: **يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ** — "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank." (58:11). Similarly, the Exalted's saying: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** — "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds." (35:10). That which ascends to Him, the Exalted, is the "good word," which is belief and knowledge. As for righteous deeds, their function is to elevate the good word and provide aid, rather than ascending to Him, the Exalted. The full explanation will come in the discussion of the verse.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of worship (العبادة), he said: **العبادة ثلاثة : قوم عبدوا الله خوفاً ، فتلك عبادة العبيد ، وقوم عبدوا الله تبارك وتعالى طلب الثواب ، فتلك عبادة الاجراء ، وقوم عبدوا الله عز وجل حباً ، فتلك عبادة الأحرار ، وهي افضل العبادة** — "Worship is of three types: a people who worship Allah out of fear, and that is the worship of slaves; a people who worship Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, seeking reward, and that is the worship of hirelings; and a people who worship Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, out of love, and that is the worship of the free, and it is the best of worship."

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: **ان قوماً عبدوا الله رغبة ، فتلك عبادة التجار ، وان قوماً عبدوا الله رهبة فتلك عبادة العبيد ، وان قوماً عبدوا الله شكراً فتلك عبادة الأحرار** — "Indeed, a people worshiped Allah out of desire, and that is the worship of merchants; and a people worshiped Allah out of dread, and that is the worship of slaves; and a people worshiped Allah out of gratitude, and that is the worship of the free."

In *al-Ilal*, *al-Majalis*, and *al-Khisal*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): **ان الناس يعبدون الله على ثلاثة اوجه : فطبقة يعبدونه رغبة في ثوابه فتلك عبادة الحرصاء وهو الطمع ، وآخرون يعبدونه خوفاً من النار فتلك عبادة العبيد ، وهي رهبة ، ولكني اعبده حباً له عز وجل فتلك عبادة الكرام ، لقوله عز وجل : « وَهُم مِّن فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ » . ولقوله عز وجل « قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ » ، فمن احب الله عز وجل احبه ، ومن احبه الله كان من الآمنين ، وهذا مقام مكنون لا يمسه إلا المطهرون** — "Indeed, people worship Allah in three ways: one group worships Him out of desire for His reward, and that is the worship of the covetous, which is greed; others worship Him out of fear of the Fire, and that is the worship of slaves, which is dread; but I worship Him out of love for Him, Mighty and Majestic is He, and that is the worship of the noble, because of His saying, Mighty and Majestic is He: 'and they shall be secure from the terror of that day' (27:89), and because of His saying, Mighty and Majestic is He: 'Say, "If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you"' (3:31). So whoever loves Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, He loves him; and whomever Allah loves is among the secure. This is a hidden station that none touch except the purified."

**I say:** The meaning of these narrations has become clear from the preceding explanation. Their (peace be upon them) description of the worship of the free sometimes as gratitude (شكراً) and sometimes as love (حباً) is because they both return to the same thing. For gratitude is to put the thing bestowed in its proper place, and the gratitude of worship is that it be for Allah who deserves it for His essence. Thus, Allah is worshiped because He is Allah—meaning because He is the possessor of all attributes of beauty and majesty in His essence. He is the Beautiful in His essence, beloved for His essence. Love is nothing but the inclination toward beauty and being attracted to it. Thus, our saying regarding Him, the Exalted, that He is worshiped because He is He, and He is worshiped because He is beautiful and beloved, and He is worshiped because He is the Bestower who is thanked through worship—all of these return to a single meaning.

It is narrated through a Sunni chain from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: إياك نعبد (You [alone] do we worship), he said: **يعني : لا نريد منك غيرك ولا نعبدك بالعوض والبدل : كما يعبدك الجاهلون بك المغيبون عنك** — "It means: we do not desire from You anything other than You, and we do not worship You for compensation or exchange, as You are worshiped by those ignorant of You, who are veiled from You."

**I say:** The narration points to what was mentioned earlier: that the meaning of worship entails presence (الحضور) and sincerity (الاخلاص) which negates the intention of exchange.

In *Tuhaf al-Uqul*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition: **ومن زعم انه يعبد بالصفة لا بالادراك فقد أحال على غائب ، ومن زعم انه يعبد الصفة والموصوف فقد أبطل التوحيد لأن الصفة غير الموصوف ، ومن زعم انه يضيف الموصوف الى الصفة فقد صغر بالكبير ، وما قدروا الله حق قدره** — "Whoever claims that he worships by the attribute (بالصفة) and not by realization (بالادراك) has referred to one who is absent; and whoever claims that he worships the attribute and the described has invalidated monotheism (التوحيد), because the attribute is other than the described; and whoever claims that he joins the described to the attribute has belittled the Great, and they have not esteemed Allah with the esteem due to Him." (The tradition continues).

In *al-Ma'ani*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of His saying: اهدنا الصراط المستقيم (Guide us on the straight path): **يعني ارشدنا الى لزوم الطريق المؤدي الى محبتك ، والمبلغ الى جنتك ، والمانع من أن نتبع اهواءنا فنعطب ، او ان نأخذ بارائنا فنهلك** — "It means: direct us to adhere to the road that leads to Your love, conveys to Your Paradise, and prevents us from following our desires so that we perish, or taking our own opinions so that we are destroyed."

Also in *al-Ma'ani*, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: **يعني ، ادم لنا توفيقك الذي اطعناك به في ماضي ايامنا ، حتى نطيعك كذلك في مستقبل اعمارنا** — "It means: continue for us Your success (توفيقك) by which we obeyed You in our past days, so that we may obey You likewise in the future of our lives."

**I say:** The two narrations represent two different ways of answering the doubt regarding the necessity of acquiring what is already possessed by seeking guidance for the one already guided. The first narration looks at the variation in the levels of guidance in their external reality (مصداقاً), while the second looks at their unity in conceptual meaning (مفهوماً).

Also in *al-Ma'ani*, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him): **الصراط المستقيم في الدنيا ما قصر عن الغلو ، وارتفع عن التقصير واستقام ، وفي الآخرة طريق المؤمنين الى الجنة** — "The straight path in this world is that which falls short of extremism (الغلو), rises above negligence (التقصير), and remains upright; and in the Hereafter, it is the road of the believers to Paradise."

Also in *al-Ma'ani*, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of "the path of those..." (صراط الذين): **اي : قولوا : اهدنا صراط الذين أنعمت عليهم بالتوفيق لدينك وطاعتك ، لا بالمال والصحة ، فانهم قد يكونون كفاراً او فساقاً ، قال : وهم الذين قال الله : « وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولَـٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولَـٰئِكَ رَفِيقًا »** — "That is: say: 'Guide us to the path of those whom You have blessed with success (التوفيق) for Your religion and Your obedience, not with wealth and health, for they might be disbelievers or transgressors.' He said: 'And they are those of whom Allah said: "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69).'"

In *al-Uyun*, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), from his fathers, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) say: 'Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, said: "I have divided the Opening of the Book (فاتحة الكتاب) between Myself and My servant; half of it is for Me and half of it is for My servant, and My servant shall have what he asks for." When the servant says: بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful), Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, says: "My servant has begun with My name, and it is incumbent upon Me to complete his affairs for him and bless him in his states." When he says: الحمد لله رب العالمين (All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds), Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, says: "My servant has praised Me and recognized that the blessings he has are from Me and that the afflictions diverted from him are by My grace; I call you to witness that I shall add the blessings of the Hereafter to his blessings in this world and divert from him the afflictions of the Hereafter as I diverted from him the afflictions of this world." When he says: الرحمن الرحيم (the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful), Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, says: "My servant has testified for Me that I am the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful; I call you to witness that I shall surely provide him an ample portion of My mercy and a generous share of My gift." When he says: مالك يوم الدين (Master of the Day of Retribution), Allah the Exalted says: "I call you to witness that just as he acknowledged that I am the Master of the Day of Retribution, I shall surely make his reckoning easy on the Day of Account, accept his good deeds, and overlook his evil deeds." When he says: إياك نعبد (You [alone] do we worship), Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, says: "My servant has spoken the truth; Me [alone] does he worship. I call you to witness that I shall surely reward him for his worship with a reward that everyone who opposed him in his worship of Me will envy." When he says: وإياك نستعين (and to You [alone] do we look for help), Allah the Exalted says: "From Me has My servant sought help and to Me has he fled for refuge; I call you to witness that I shall surely help him in his affair, aid him in his hardships, and take his hand on the day of his calamities." When he says: إهدنا الصراط المستقيم (Guide us on the straight path) to the end of the Surah, Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, says: "This is for My servant, and My servant shall have what he asks for; I have answered My servant, given him what he hoped for, and secured him from that which he feared."'"

**I say:** Al-Saduq narrated something similar in *al-Ilal* from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him). The narration, as you see, explains Surah al-Fatiha within the prayer (الصلوة). It supports what has been mentioned repeatedly: that the Surah is a speech belonging to Him, the Glorified, acting as a proxy for His servant in what he mentions in the station of worship and the manifestation of servitude—from the praise of his Lord to the manifestation of his worship. It is a Surah designed for worship, and there is no Surah in the Quran that parallels it in its function. By that, I mean:

**First:** The Surah in its entirety is a speech spoken by Allah, the Glorified, in the station of proxy for His servant in what he says when he turns his face toward the station of Lordship and places himself in the station of servitude.

**Second:** It is divided into two parts: half for Allah and half for the servant.

**Third:** It contains all the Quranic knowledges despite its brevity and conciseness. For the Quran, in its wondrous breadth of fundamental knowledges and the branches that stem from them—including ethics, rulings on worship, transactions, politics, social matters, promises, threats, stories, and lessons—returns in its collective explanations to monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد) and their branches, and to the guidance of servants to what rectifies their worldly and otherworldly affairs. This Surah, as is clear, contains all of these in the briefest wording and clearest meaning.

You should compare what is manifest to you of the beauty of this Surah, which Allah the Glorified has placed in the prayer of the Muslims, with what the Christians place in their prayer from the speech found in the Gospel of Matthew (6:9-13), which we mention in its Arabic wording: "Our Father who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. Amen."

Reflect on the meanings conveyed by the words of these sentences as "heavenly knowledges" and what they contain of the etiquette of servitude. It mentions first that their father (who is Allah, hallowed be His name) is in the heavens!! Then it supplicates for the Father that His name be hallowed, His kingdom come, and His will be carried out on earth as it is in heaven—but who is to answer this supplication, which resembles the slogans of political parties? Then it asks Allah to give the bread of the day and to meet forgiveness with forgiveness, making the overlooking of a right contingent upon their own overlooking. And what right would they have if Allah had not granted them a right? It asks Him not to test them but to save them from the evil one—and that is impossible, for this world is the abode of testing and perfection; what is the meaning of salvation without trial and testing? Then, let your wonder be complete at what was mentioned by some Orientalists among the scholars of the West, followed by some who affiliate themselves [with Islam]: that Islam does not surpass others in knowledges, for all the Sharias of Allah call to monotheism and the purification of souls through virtuous character and righteous deeds, and religions only differ in the deep-rootedness of their social fruits!!

## (Another Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Faqih* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: **الصراط المستقيم أمير المؤمنين عليه‌السلام** — "The straight path (الصراط المستقيم) is the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him)."

In *al-Ma‘ani*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "It is the path toward the gnosis (معرفة) of Allah. There are two paths: a path in this world and a path in the Hereafter. As for the path in this world, it is the Imam whose obedience is obligatory (المفترض الطاعة). Whoever recognizes him in this world and follows his guidance shall pass over the path that is the bridge over Hell in the Hereafter; but whoever does not recognize him in this world, his foot shall slip in the Hereafter and he will tumble into the fire of Hell."

Also in *al-Ma‘ani*, narrated from Imam al-Sajjad (عليه السلام), he said: "There is no veil between Allah and His Proof (حجته), nor is there any screen for Allah excluding His Proof. We are the gates of Allah, we are the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), we are the receptacle of His knowledge (عيبة علمه), we are the interpreters of His revelation (تراجمة وحيه), we are the pillars of His monotheism (أركان توحيده), and we are the location of His secret."

Narrated by Ibn Shahrashub from the *Tafsir* of Waki‘ ibn al-Jarrah, from al-Thawri, from al-Suddi, from Asbat and Mujahid, from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding the saying of the Exalted: **اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** "Guide us on the straight path," he said: "Say, O assembly of servants: 'Guide us toward the love of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام).'"

**I say:** There are other narrations conveying these same meanings. These reports belong to the category of "flow" (الجري) — *the principle of applying a Quranic verse to its ongoing instances and extensions* — and the identification of the foremost referent (المصداق) of the verse. Know that "flow" (الجري) — a term we shall frequently employ in this book — is a technical term derived from the speech of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام).

__________________

(1) The virtuous priest Gustave Le Bon in *The Civilization of the Arabs*.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) about this narration: "There is no verse in the Quran but that it has an outer aspect (ظهر) and an inner aspect (بطن), and there is no letter in it but that it has a limit (حد), and for every limit there is a point of ascent (مُطَّلَع)." I asked: "What is meant by its outer and inner aspects?" He (عليه السلام) replied: "Its outer aspect is its revelation (تنزيله) and its inner aspect is its esoteric interpretation (تأويله). Some of it has already passed, and some of it has not yet come to be. It flows (يجري) just as the sun and the moon flow; whenever something of it arrives, the discourse occurs."

There are other narrations with this same meaning. This is the methodology (سليقة) of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام); they apply a verse of the Quran to any instance (الموارد) that is capable of being its referent, even if it lies outside the specific occasion of revelation (مورد النزول). Rational consideration (الاعتبار) supports this, for the Quran was sent down as guidance for all the worlds (العالمين), guiding them toward the necessary belief (واجب الاعتقاد), the necessary character (واجب الخلق), and the necessary action (واجب العمل). The theoretical knowledge (المعارف النظرية) it clarifies consists of realities (حقائق) that are not restricted to one state over another, nor one time over another. Similarly, any virtue or vice it mentions, or any practical ruling (حكم عملي) it legislates, is not confined to one individual over another or one era over another, due to the universality of legislation (عموم التشريع).

Furthermore, the reported occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) — *the specific event or circumstance that prompted the descent of a verse or verses regarding a person or incident* — does not necessitate restricting the ruling to that specific incident such that the ruling expires when the incident concludes or dies with the death of the person involved. This is because the exposition is general (عام) and the underlying reason (التعليل) is absolute (مطلق). Indeed, the praise revealed regarding certain believers, or the condemnation revealed regarding others based on the presence of specific traits in them, cannot be restricted to the person who was the occasion of revelation when those very same traits exist in another people after them, and so on. The Quran also indicates this; the Exalted says: **يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوَانَهُ** "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure" (al-Ma'idah: 16). And He says: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it" (Fussilat: 42). And the Exalted says: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ** "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (al-Hijr: 9).

The narrations concerning the application of Quranic verses to them (the Imams, عليه السلام) or to their enemies — meaning the narrations of "flow" (الجري) — are numerous across various chapters, perhaps reaching into the hundreds. Following this general notification, we shall omit the citation of most of them in the discussions on narrations (الأبحاث الروائية), as they fall outside the primary objective of this book, except where a specific purpose in the discussion requires it; so let this be remembered.
